title: 10 possible side effects of Tanacetum vulgare (tansy)

side_effects_list.side_effect_desc: Tanacetum vulgare causes allergic reactions due to its potential to trigger an immune response. The plant's sap and essential oils contain compounds that can bind to proteins on skin cells, stimulating the release of histamine and other chemical mediators. This leads to symptoms such as hives, itching, redness, and swelling in some individuals who are sensitive to tansy.

Tanacetum vulgare triggers asthma attacks due to its natural ability to stimulate bronchial tubes and increase airway constriction. The presence of sesquiterpene lactones, a specific class of compounds found in the plant, can cause an allergic reaction in some individuals, leading to inflammation and constriction of airways, which may trigger asthma attacks.

Tanacetum vulgare induces liver damage when used in excessive amounts or for extended periods of time. The volatile oils present in the plant, particularly camphor and thujone, are toxic to the liver and can cause damage to its cells and tissues, leading to inflammation and scarring. This damage can result in a range of symptoms including nausea, fatigue, and abdominal pain, and may even progress to more severe complications if left untreated.

Tanacetum vulgare produces gastrointestinal upset in some individuals. The herb contains sesquiterpene lactones, which can cause stomach discomfort and diarrhea due to their irritating effect on the mucous membranes of the digestive tract. Additionally, Tanacetum vulgare's coumarins have been shown to increase gut motility, leading to symptoms like nausea, bloating, and abdominal cramps in susceptible individuals.

Tanacetum vulgare increases risk of bleeding due to its ability to inhibit platelet aggregation and prolong bleeding time. This is attributed to the presence of sesquiterpene lactones, particularly tanetin, which have been shown to exhibit anticoagulant properties. Additionally, tansy's potential interaction with blood thinners and its ability to stimulate the breakdown of clotting factors also contribute to this adverse effect.

Tanacetum vulgare causes skin irritation in some individuals. The sesquiterpene lactones and coumarins present in its essential oil are believed to be responsible for this adverse effect. These compounds can cause contact dermatitis, characterized by redness, itching, and blisters on the skin. Additionally, the flavonoids and phenolic acids may also contribute to the irritating properties of Tanacetum vulgare, leading to skin discomfort.

Tanacetum vulgare triggers kidney problems due to its ability to interfere with the normal functioning of the kidneys. The herb's potent compounds can cause a decrease in urine production, leading to a buildup of toxins and waste products in the body. This can put additional strain on the kidneys, potentially exacerbating existing issues or even causing new problems.

Tanacetum vulgare lowers blood pressure by interfering with the body's ability to constrict blood vessels and increasing the activity of nitric oxide, a molecule that relaxes smooth muscle cells. This can lead to a decrease in blood pressure, particularly if used in large quantities or for extended periods. The flavonoids present in Tanacetum vulgare are thought to be responsible for this effect, as they have been shown to exhibit vasodilatory properties and antagonize the action of vasoconstrictors.

Tanacetum vulgare interacts with medications by altering their absorption and effectiveness. The compound chamazulene in tansy may reduce the absorption of certain medications, such as warfarin, and increase the risk of bleeding or other adverse effects. Additionally, tansy's flavonoids may interact with blood thinners, antiplatelet agents, and anticoagulant medications, potentially leading to increased bleeding risk or reduced medication efficacy.

Tanacetum vulgare causes headaches due to its ability to interact with the body's natural chemicals and cause a temporary imbalance in neurotransmitters. This disruption can lead to discomfort and pain in the head and neck area. The herb's active compounds, including camphor and borneol, may contribute to this effect by stimulating nerve endings and causing blood vessel constriction or dilation. As a result, some individuals may experience recurring headaches when using Tanacetum vulgare.

intro: Tanacetum vulgare has some side effects when used improperly, such as nausea, vomiting, and allergic reactions. These side effects can occur due to the herb's volatile oils and sesquiterpenes, which can cause digestive issues and skin irritation. For instance, a person experiencing severe nausea may struggle with daily activities, while an allergic reaction could lead to respiratory issues, making simple tasks like walking or breathing difficult.
intro_desc: Tanacetum vulgare has some side effects when used improperly, such as gastrointestinal issues, dizziness, and allergic reactions. These side effects can be caused by ingesting large quantities or using it in combination with other medications. For example, individuals with sensitive stomachs may experience severe abdominal pain, diarrhea, or vomiting, which can lead to dehydration and impact daily activities. Additionally, allergic reactions can range from mild itching to life-threatening anaphylaxis, causing people to miss work, school, or important events.
